A brief overview of cooperative game theory is provided in various books
Solution concepts in cooperative game theory aim to determine the allocation of utility achieved by the grand coalition of agents.
Cooperative game theory involves a set of agents or players represented by a set N.
Cooperative game theory includes a characteristic function v that maps coalitions to real numbers indicating joint rewards obtained by the players.
Weighted voting games are a restricted class of simple cooperative games.
In weighted voting games, each agent i has a weight wi that contributes to the overall sum determining if a team of agents wins.
In weighted voting games, a coalition is deemed a losing coalition if the sum of weights does not exceed a quota q, and a winning coalition if it does.
Weighted voting games have been studied as a model for voting bodies such as the US Electoral College or the EU Council of Ministers.
The characteristic function in cooperative game theory defines the joint value of a coalition formed by agents.
Solution concepts in cooperative game theory focus on determining the allocation of utility achieved by the grand coalition of agents.
p is an allocation of the utility v(N) achieved by the grand coalition of all the agents
p is called an imputation if for any player i we have pi ≥ 0 and ∑pi = v(N)
Imputation in terms of player i such that pi ≥ 0

Rational players may abandon the grand coalition of all the agents if they can increase their individual utility by doing so. The Core is defined as the set of all payoff vectors where no subset of agents can generate more utility, as measured by the characteristic function, than the total payoff they are currently awarded by the payoff vector
The Core is viewed as the set of stable payoff allocations
The ε-Core is a relaxation of the core
Some games have empty cores, meaning that no payoff allocations achieve full stability
In such cases, researchers have proposed minimizing the instability
The ε-core consists of imputations where for any value coalition C we have p(C) ≥ v(C) − ε. This indicates the relationship between the concept of ε-core and imputations.

The difference v(C) − Σi∈C pi is called the excess of the coalition, representing the total improvement in utility the members of C can achieve by abandoning the grand coalition and working on their own. This shows the relationship between the concepts of coalition and excess.

For an imputation in the ε-core, no agent subset C can achieve an addition of ε in utility over the current total payoff offered to the team. This highlights the relationship between the concepts of agent subset and utility.
The minimal ε for which the ε-core is non-empty is called the Least-Core Value (LCV). This indicates the relationship between the concept of Least-Core Value and ε-core.

The LCV minimizes the incentive of any agent subset to abandon the grand coalition. The LCV thus represents the degree of instability (excess) under the imputation that best minimizes this instability. This shows the relationship between the concepts of Least-Core Value and instability.

Two power indices, payoff distributions reflecting the true influence a player has on the performance of the team, are discussed. This indicates the relationship between the concepts of power indices and payoff distributions.

The Shapley Value measures the average marginal contribution of each player across all permutations of the players. This highlights the relationship between the concepts of Shapley Value and average marginal contribution.

A ground-truth dataset is generated by setting the number of samples in the SHAP package to 5000 samples.
The number of samples in the KernelExplainer determines the number of times a model is re-evaluated for every prediction.
A higher number of samples will result in more accurate (and lower variance in the) Shapley values, but require a longer compute time.
The inference time scales approximately linearly with the number of re-evaluation samples.

To contrast the difference in computation time, Shapley values are computed on 10 % of the data, the neural network is trained in 30 seconds, and Shapley values are predicted on the remaining 90% of the data.

The whole procedure takes roughly 12 % of the time it took to compute Shapley values for the entire dataset, speeding-up by 8x while keeping a reasonable prediction quality on the remaining unseen data.
